content.assessment: The Finnish comprehensive-school experience is a story of active structural reform paired with sustained institutional investment; it offers no support for a passive monitoring-only approach. Hungary's tracking age (10) and selection intensity (85% vs OECD 43%) are far outside anything Finland tolerated even before its 1972-1977 reform, and Finland's own post-1990s experience shows that even comprehensive systems require continual institutional maintenance (guardrails) to prevent re-segregation. A status quo approach, by definition, provides none of this maintenance and leaves Hungary's already-extreme tracking and segregation indicators unaddressed.
opportunities: Monitoring infrastructure could, in principle, be repurposed later to evaluate any future comprehensive-style reform
strengths: Avoids the political durability risk seen in reversed reforms elsewhere, since no structural change is attempted
threats: Finland's own post-1990s re-segregation under laissez-faire choice conditions shows that a lack of active structural intervention (as in status quo) tends toward worsening segregation over time, particularly under demographic and geographic pressures analogous to those cited for Hungary
weaknesses: Finland's positive trajectory required active de-tracking plus simultaneous systemic reforms, not passive monitoring, so the lens offers no mechanism by which monitoring alone would replicate Finnish gains
Hungary's tracking age and selection share are already far more extreme than pre-reform Finland's two-tier system, suggesting the comprehensive-school lens implies more urgency for change, not less
